parts.title: The Designer's Mind
parts.tag: How your own psychology shapes what you design

parts.title: Minding the Design
parts.tag: How interface mechanics shape perception and behavior

parts.title: The User's Mind
parts.tag: What users bring psychologically before they ever touch your product

parts.title: The Organization's Mind
parts.tag: How the system you work inside shapes what gets shipped

chapters.title: Introduction
chapters.subtitle: Why I wrote this book and what you are about to read

chapters.title: Nobody Thinks Like You
chapters.subtitle: How false consensus makes you design for yourself
chapters.tldr: False consensus makes you think users think like you. They do not. Your preference feels like evidence because your brain keeps using itself as the sample size.

refs.title: Ross, L., Greene, D., & House, P. (1977). The “false consensus effect”: An egocentric bias in social perception and attribution processes.
Bosveld, W., Koomen, W., & van der Pligt, J. (1994). Selective exposure and the false consensus effect: The availability of similar and dissimilar others.

Wikipedia: Color Labs.

McHugh, M. (2011). Where did Color go wrong?
The Decision Lab: False Consensus Effect.
Simply Psychology: False Consensus Effect.

chapters.title: You Ruin Your Own Designs
chapters.subtitle: How imposter syndrome makes you risk-averse
chapters.tldr: Impostor syndrome does not stay in your head. It leaks into the work. You play safe, shrink the idea, and the user gets the timid version instead of the bold one.

refs.title: Bravata, D. M., Watts, S. A., Keefer, A. L., Madhusudhan, D. K., Taylor, K. T., Clark, D. M., Nelson, R. S., Cokley, K. O., & Hagg, H. K. (2020). Prevalence, Predictors, and Treatment of Impostor Syndrome: A Systematic Review.

Fried, J., & Heinemeier Hansson, D. (2010). Rework.
Lane, J. A. (2015). The Imposter Phenomenon Among Emerging Adults Transitioning Into Professional Life.
Want, J., & Kleitman, S. (2006). Imposter phenomenon and self-handicapping: Links with parenting styles and self-confidence.
Noskeau, R., Santos, A., & Wang, W. (2021). Connecting the Dots Between Mindset and Impostor Phenomenon, via Fear of Failure and Goal Orientation, in Working Adults.
Superfried (2024). Top 10 Tips: Designers & Imposter Syndrome.
Wikipedia: Impostor syndrome.
Young, V. (2011). The Secret Thoughts of Successful Women.
Basecamp: Where we came from.

chapters.title: Design for Your Ego
chapters.subtitle: How cognitive dissonance turns feedback into a threat
chapters.tldr: Cognitive dissonance makes criticism feel personal. Once your identity gets tied up in the work, feedback stops being useful input and starts feeling like something you need to defend against.

refs.title: Festinger, L. (1957). A Theory of Cognitive Dissonance.
Kahan, D. M. (2013). Ideology, motivated reasoning, and cognitive reflection.
Tavris, C., & Aronson, E. (2007). Mistakes Were Made (But Not by Me).
Wikipedia: Cognitive dissonance.
Nielsen Norman Group: How to Overcome Confirmation Bias in UX Research.
Simply Psychology: Cognitive Dissonance.
The Decision Lab: Confirmation Bias.

chapters.title: Your Mind Lies to You
chapters.subtitle: How the brain defends your work while pretending to evaluate it
chapters.tldr: The longer you work on something, the less clearly you see it. Familiarity, overconfidence, and false understanding make your own design feel better and clearer than it really is.

refs.title: Reber, R., Schwarz, N., & Winkielman, P. (2004). Processing fluency and aesthetic pleasure: Is beauty in the perceiver’s processing experience?
Nickerson, R. S. (1998). Confirmation bias: A ubiquitous phenomenon in many guises.
Rozenblit, L., & Keil, F. (2002). The misunderstood limits of folk science: An illusion of explanatory depth.
Kruger, J., & Dunning, D. (1999). Unskilled and unaware of it.
refs.venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 77(6), 1121–1134

refs.title: Wikipedia: Dunning-Kruger effect.
Nielsen Norman Group: Confirmation Bias in UX Research.

chapters.title: Taste Isn't Talent
chapters.subtitle: How broad exposure builds taste and narrow exposure fakes it
chapters.tldr: Real taste comes from seeing a lot of good work over time. The mere exposure effect does something else: it makes your own design feel good just because you have looked at it too long.

refs.title: Zajonc, R. B. (1980). Feeling and thinking: Preferences need no inferences.
Bornstein, R. F. (1989). Exposure and affect: Overview and meta-analysis of research, 1968–1987.

Lovell, S. (2011). Dieter Rams: As Little Design as Possible.

chapters.title: Creativity Isn't Magic
chapters.subtitle: Why creativity is deliberate practice, not inspiration
chapters.tldr: \ Divergent thinking does not happen in your head while you wait. It happens when you make something rough, react to it, and improve it. Creativity comes from the work, not from waiting for inspiration.

refs.title: Guilford, J. P. (1950). Creativity.
Amabile, T. M. (1983). The social psychology of creativity: A componential conceptualization.
refs.venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 45(2), 357–376

refs.title: Ericsson, K. A., Krampe, R. T., & Tesch-Römer, C. (1993). The role of deliberate practice in the acquisition of expert performance.

Jobs, S. (1996). Steve Jobs: The Next Insanely Great Thing.

Wikipedia: IDEO.
Nielsen Norman Group: Ideation for Everyday Design Challenges.
The Decision Lab: Divergent Thinking.

chapters.title: Deadlines Make You Dumb
chapters.subtitle: How pressure narrows your thinking at exactly the wrong moment
chapters.tldr: The Yerkes-Dodson law shows that pressure only helps up to a point. After that, stress narrows your thinking, makes risks feel inconvenient, and pushes teams toward the fastest answer instead of the right one.

refs.title: Yerkes, R. M., & Dodson, J. D. (1908). The relation of strength of stimulus to rapidity of habit-formation.
refs.venue: Journal of Comparative Neurology and Psychology, 18(5), 459–482

refs.title: Staw, B. M., Sandelands, L. E., & Dutton, J. E. (1981). Threat-rigidity effects in organizational behavior: A multilevel analysis.
Amabile, T. M., Mueller, J. S., Simpson, W. B., Hadley, C. N., Kramer, S. J., & Fleming, L. (2002). Time pressure and creativity in organizations.
Wikipedia: Yerkes-Dodson law.
Wikipedia: Space Shuttle Challenger disaster.
NASA Rogers Commission Report, Volume I, Chapter 5 (1986).
Simply Psychology: Yerkes-Dodson Law.

chapters.title: Love at First Sketch
chapters.subtitle: How design fixation traps you in your first idea
chapters.tldr: Design fixation makes your first idea feel better than it is. Once that first sketch is on the page, most of your “exploration” becomes small variations on it instead of real alternatives.

refs.title: Jansson, D. G., & Smith, S. M. (1991). Design fixation.
Luchins, A. S. (1942). Mechanization in problem solving: The effect of Einstellung.
Chrysikou, E. G., & Weisberg, R. W. (2005). Following the wrong footsteps: Fixation effects of pictorial examples in a design problem-solving task.

refs.title: Wikipedia: Functional fixedness.
Fessenden, T. (2018). The Anchoring Principle.
Wikipedia: Zune.
Your

chapters.title: Your Knowledge Is the Problem
chapters.subtitle: Why experts judge their own work worst
chapters.tldr: The curse of knowledge makes experts terrible judges of their own work. Once you know how something works, you stop seeing what a new user sees and start filling in the gaps automatically.

refs.title: Camerer, C., Loewenstein, G., & Weber, M. (1989). The curse of knowledge in economic settings: An experimental analysis.
Heath, C., & Heath, D. (2006). The curse of knowledge.
Wieman, C. E. (2007). The "curse of knowledge," or why intuition about teaching often fails.
Wikipedia: Curse of knowledge.
The Decision Lab: Curse of Knowledge.
Nielsen Norman Group: Evaluate Interface Learnability with Cognitive Walkthroughs.
Adobe Blog (2015). Dreams from the Digital Darkroom: 25 Years of Photoshop.
Adobe Blog (2015). Adobe Explains It All: Photoshop.
Adobe. Photoshop Elements 2026.

chapters.title: You Are Not Listening
chapters.subtitle: How confirmation bias filters feedback before it lands
chapters.tldr: Confirmation bias shapes what feedback you notice, trust, and explain away. If you do not decide in advance what would prove you wrong, research ends up backing what you already believed.

refs.title: Nickerson, R. S. (1998). Confirmation bias: A ubiquitous phenomenon in many guises.
Wason, P. C. (1960). On the failure to eliminate hypotheses in a conceptual task.
Dweck, C. S. (2006). Mindset: The New Psychology of Success.
Wikipedia: Confirmation bias.
Nielsen Norman Group: Confirmation Bias in UX.
Simply Psychology: Confirmation Bias in Psychology.
Wikipedia: Segway.

chapters.title: Pass the Vibe Check First
chapters.subtitle: How first impressions form faster than conscious thought
chapters.tldr: Thin-slicing means users judge your product before they use it. In a split second, the look of the interface signals trust, quality, and competence, and that first impression shapes everything that follows.

refs.title: Ambady, N., & Rosenthal, R. (1992). Thin slices of expressive behavior as predictors of interpersonal consequences: A meta-analysis.
Lindgaard, G., Fernandes, G., Dudek, C., & Brown, J. (2006). Attention web designers: You have 50 milliseconds to make a good first impression!
Willis, J., & Todorov, A. (2006). First impressions: Making up your mind after a 100-ms exposure to a face.
Wikipedia: Thin-slicing.
Nielsen Norman Group: How Long Do Users Stay on Web Pages?
Nielsen Norman Group: The Aesthetic-Usability Effect.
Simply Psychology: Thin-Slicing and First Impressions.

chapters.title: Your Design Is Rigged
chapters.subtitle: How defaults and framing steer behavior silently
chapters.tldr: The default effect and framing effect mean users do not choose from a neutral screen. They follow the option that looks normal, recommended, or easiest to accept, often without noticing they were steered there.

refs.title: Tversky, A., & Kahneman, D. (1981). The framing of decisions and the psychology of choice.
Johnson, E. J., & Goldstein, D. (2003). Do defaults save lives?
Thaler, R. H., & Sunstein, C. R. (2008). Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness.
Wikipedia: Default effect (psychology).
Wikipedia: Choice architecture.
Nielsen Norman Group: Choice Architecture and Defaults.
The Decision Lab: Default Effect.

chapters.title: Stop Hiding What's Clickable
chapters.subtitle: Why buttons need to look like buttons
chapters.tldr: Affordances make actions possible, but signifiers show users where those actions are. When you remove the visual cues that say “tap here” or “click this,” usability drops fast no matter how clean the design looks.

refs.title: Gibson, J. J. (1979). The Ecological Approach to Visual Perception.
Norman, D. A. (1988). The Psychology of Everyday Things.
Norman, D. A. (1999). Affordances, conventions, and design.
Wikipedia: Affordance.
Nielsen Norman Group: Signifiers, Not Affordances.
Interaction Design Foundation: Affordances.

chapters.title: Intuitive Design Is a Lie
chapters.subtitle: Why intuitive just means familiar
chapters.tldr: What people call "intuitive" is usually schema recognition. A design feels intuitive when it matches patterns users already know, and confusing when it asks them to learn a new one on the spot.

refs.title: Bartlett, F. C. (1932). Remembering: A Study in Experimental and Social Psychology.
Klein, G. (1998). Sources of Power: How People Make Decisions.
Chase, W. G., & Simon, H. A. (1973). Perception in chess.
Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive load during problem solving: Effects on learning.
Wikipedia: Schema (psychology).
Wikipedia: Recognition-primed decision.
Nielsen Norman Group: Hamburger Menus and Hidden Navigation Hurt UX Metrics.
Interaction Design Foundation: Recognition-Primed Decision Making.

chapters.title: Your UI Is Exhausting
chapters.subtitle: Why every extra thing on screen costs the user something
chapters.tldr: Cognitive load builds up faster than teams expect. Every extra label, decision, and unnecessary step takes up working memory, and at some point users stop using your product because it feels like too much work.

refs.title: Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive load during problem solving: Effects on learning.
Cowan, N. (2001). The magical number 4 in short-term memory: A reconsideration of mental storage capacity.
Paas, F., Renkl, A., & Sweller, J. (2003). Cognitive load theory and instructional design: Recent developments.
Wikipedia: Cognitive load.
Krug, S. (2000). Don't Make Me Think: A Common Sense Approach to Web Usability.
Nielsen Norman Group: Minimize Cognitive Load to Maximize Usability.
Interaction Design Foundation: Cognitive Load Theory.

chapters.title: Stop Breaking the Pattern
chapters.subtitle: Why breaking your own patterns confuses the people using it
chapters.tldr: Predictive processing means users are always guessing what comes next. When your interface keeps changing its own patterns, people slow down, trust it less, and spend more effort checking things they should not have to check.

refs.title: Berger, C. R., & Calabrese, R. J. (1975). Some explorations in initial interaction and beyond.
Clark, A. (2016). Surfing Uncertainty: Prediction, Action, and the Embodied Mind.
Lee, J. D., & See, K. A. (2004). Trust in automation: Designing for appropriate reliance.
Wikipedia: Predictive coding.
Nielsen Norman Group: Consistency and Standards.
Wikipedia: User Account Control.

chapters.title: Nobody Remembers Your UI
chapters.subtitle: How memory decay shapes every interaction
chapters.tldr: The forgetting curve means most users do not remember your interface well enough to rely on memory. Show people what they need instead of expecting them to recall it.

refs.title: Tulving, E., & Thomson, D. M. (1973). Encoding specificity and retrieval processes in episodic memory.
Ebbinghaus, H. (1885/1913). Memory: A Contribution to Experimental Psychology.
Godden, D. R., & Baddeley, A. D. (1975). Context-dependent memory in two natural environments: On land and underwater.
Wikipedia: Forgetting curve.
Wikipedia: Encoding specificity principle.
Nielsen Norman Group: Recognition and Recall in UX.
Schneier, B. (2016). Stop Trying to Fix the User.

chapters.title: Design the Last Moment First
chapters.subtitle: Why the last thing users experience is the only thing they remember
chapters.tldr: The peak-end rule means users do not remember an experience as a full average. They mostly remember the most intense moment and the ending, so weak endings and bad peaks can ruin good work.

refs.title: Fredrickson, B. L., & Kahneman, D. (1993). Duration neglect in retrospective evaluations of affective episodes.
refs.venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 65(1), 45–55

refs.title: Kahneman, D., Fredrickson, B. L., Schreiber, C. A., & Redelmeier, D. A. (1993). When more pain is preferred to less: Adding a better end.
Zakay, D., & Block, R. A. (1997). Temporal cognition.
Wikipedia: Peak-end rule.
Nielsen Norman Group: Response Times: The 3 Important Limits.
UX Collective: Designing for Wait: How to Improve Perceived Performance.
Wikipedia: Disney FastPass.

chapters.title: Fake Progress Is Real Motivation
chapters.subtitle: Why showing progress makes people push harder to finish
chapters.tldr: The goal-gradient effect means people work harder when they feel close to done. A progress bar does not just show progress. It can change motivation by making users feel they have already started and should keep going.

refs.title: Nunes, J. C., & Drèze, X. (2006). The endowed progress effect: How artificial advancement increases effort.
Hull, C. L. (1932). The goal-gradient hypothesis and maze learning.
Kivetz, R., Urminsky, O., & Zheng, Y. (2006). The goal-gradient hypothesis resurrected: Purchase acceleration, illusionary goal progress, and customer retention.
Wikipedia: Goal-gradient hypothesis.
Nielsen Norman Group: Progress Indicators Make a Slow System Less Insufferable.
UX Collective: The Psychology of Progress Bars.

chapters.title: Layout Speaks Before You Do
chapters.subtitle: How the brain organizes your layout before you explain it
chapters.tldr: Gestalt principles mean users read structure before content. Spacing, alignment, similarity, and grouping already tell them what belongs together and what matters most, before they read a word.

refs.title: Wertheimer, M. (1923). Laws of organization in perceptual forms.
refs.venue: In W. D. Ellis (Ed.), A Source Book of Gestalt Psychology. Harcourt, Brace

refs.title: Köhler, W. (1929). Gestalt Psychology.
Koffka, K. (1935). Principles of Gestalt Psychology.
Wikipedia: Gestalt psychology.
Nielsen Norman Group: Gestalt Principles of Perception.
Simply Psychology: Gestalt Laws of Perceptual Organization.

chapters.title: Users Don't Think in Tasks
chapters.subtitle: Why users don't think in tasks, they think in outcomes
chapters.tldr: Goal-setting theory shows that people organize their behavior around outcomes, not interface tasks. Users do not care about completing your flow unless it gets them closer to the change they actually want.

refs.title: Locke, E. A., & Latham, G. P. (2002). Building a practically useful theory of goal setting and task motivation: A 35-year odyssey.
Ryan, R. M., & Deci, E. L. (2000). Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being.
Christensen, C. M., Hall, T., Dillon, K., & Duncan, D. S. (2016). Competing Against Luck: The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice.
Wikipedia: Goal theory.
Wikipedia: Jobs to be done theory.
Nielsen Norman Group: Task Analysis.
Interaction Design Foundation: Jobs-to-be-Done Framework.

chapters.title: Users Want Now, Not Later
chapters.subtitle: Why users always want the reward now, not later
chapters.tldr: Present bias and hyperbolic discounting make a small reward now feel stronger than a bigger reward later. If your product asks users to wait too long before they feel value, most of them will leave.

refs.title: Laibson, D. (1997). Golden eggs and hyperbolic discounting.
O'Donoghue, T., & Rabin, M. (1999). Doing it now or later.
Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect theory: An analysis of decision under risk.
Wikipedia: Hyperbolic discounting.
Wikipedia: Present bias.
The Decision Lab: Hyperbolic Discounting.
Dredge, S. (2014). Spotify CEO speaks out on Taylor Swift albums removal: 'I'm really frustrated'.

chapters.title: Your Users Are Lying to You
chapters.subtitle: Why what users say they'll do and what they do are different things
chapters.tldr: The intention-behavior gap means people often mean what they say and still do something else. Research captures intentions in a calm moment, but your product meets users later, in real life, where friction and habit usually win.

refs.title: Sheeran, P. (2002). Intention-behaviour relations: A conceptual and empirical review.
Sheeran, P., & Webb, T. L. (2016). The intention-behavior gap.
Wikipedia: Social desirability bias.
Wikipedia: Intention-behavior gap.
Nielsen Norman Group: Interviewing Users: Stop Asking What They Want.
Wikipedia: Jawbone (company).

chapters.title: Users React, Then Rationalize
chapters.subtitle: Why users feel your product before they think about it
chapters.tldr: Affective primacy means users feel your product before they understand it. That first emotional reaction shapes the judgment that comes after, and by then the rational explanation is mostly catching up.

refs.title: Zajonc, R. B. (1980). Feeling and thinking: Preferences need no inferences.
Damasio, A. R. (1994). Descartes' Error: Emotion, Reason, and the Human Brain.
LeDoux, J. (1996). The Emotional Brain: The Mysterious Underpinnings of Emotional Life.
Wikipedia: Affective primacy.
Wikipedia: Somatic marker hypothesis.
Nielsen Norman Group: The Role of Emotion in UX Design.
Wikipedia: Clippit.

chapters.title: Users Will Hate Your New Design
chapters.subtitle: How loss aversion makes every update feel like theft
chapters.tldr: Loss aversion, status quo bias, and reactance all work against redesigns. Users do not judge the new version on its own. They judge it against what they feel you took away.

refs.title: Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect theory: An analysis of decision under risk.
Brehm, J. W. (1966). A Theory of Psychological Reactance.
Samuelson, W., & Zeckhauser, R. (1988). Status quo bias in decision making.

Wikipedia: Reactance (psychology).
Nielsen Norman Group: Prospect Theory and Loss Aversion: How Users Make Decisions.
Wikipedia: Snapchat.
Chapter 26

chapters.title: Your Design Doesn't Translate
chapters.subtitle: How culture shapes what users see, trust, and understand
chapters.tldr: Cultural cognition means users do not all read the same screen the same way. What feels clear, complete, or trustworthy in one culture can feel empty, confusing, or wrong in another.

refs.title: Nisbett, R. E., Peng, K., Choi, I., & Norenzayan, A. (2001). Culture and systems of thought: Holistic versus analytic cognition.
Henrich, J., Heine, S. J., & Norenzayan, A. (2010). The weirdest people in the world?
Marcus, A., & Gould, E. W. (2000). Crosscurrents: Cultural dimensions and global web user-interface design.
Hall, E. T. (1976). Beyond Culture.
Nielsen Norman Group: Modify Your Design for Global Audiences.
Wikipedia: WeChat.

chapters.title: Old Habits Beat Better Products
chapters.subtitle: How habit loops make existing behavior unbeatable
chapters.tldr: The endowment effect and habit loops make switching much harder than teams expect. Users are not choosing between two products on a clean slate. They are being pulled toward the one they already own, know, and use without thinking.

refs.title: Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J. L., & Thaler, R. H. (1991). Anomalies: The endowment effect, loss aversion, and status quo bias.
Wood, W., & Neal, D. T. (2007). A new look at habits and the habit-goal interface.
Gourville, J. T. (2006). Eager sellers and stony buyers: Understanding the psychology of new-product adoption.
Wikipedia: Endowment effect.
Wikipedia: Status quo bias.
Nielsen Norman Group: Why Does a Better Product Lose?
Wikipedia: Betamax.

chapters.title: More Options Make Users Quit
chapters.subtitle: Why adding features makes your product harder to use
chapters.tldr: Feature fatigue and Hick’s law mean more options usually make products harder to choose, learn, and use. Extra features may help you win the comparison table, but they often make the real experience worse.

refs.title: Iyengar, S. S., & Lepper, M. R. (2000). When choice is demotivating: Can one desire too much of a good thing?
refs.venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 79(6), 995–1006.  [

refs.title: Schwartz, B. (2004). The Paradox of Choice: Why More Is Less.
Hick, W. E. (1952). On the rate of gain of information.
Thompson, D. V., Hamilton, R. W., & Rust, R. T. (2005). Feature fatigue: When product capabilities become too much of a good thing.
Pendo. (2019). The 2019 Feature Adoption Report.
Wikipedia: Paradox of choice.
Wikipedia: Hick's law.
Maeda, J. (2006). The Laws of Simplicity.
Nielsen Norman Group: Simplicity Wins over Abundance of Choice.

chapters.title: Users Will Ignore You
chapters.subtitle: Why users ignore most of what you build
chapters.tldr: Attention is limited, and your product is competing with everything else in a user’s day. If something is not clearly worth their attention right now, their brain will filter it out before they even think about it.

refs.title: Kahneman, D. (1973). Attention and Effort.
Strayer, D. L., & Johnston, W. A. (2001). Driven to distraction: Dual-task studies of simulated driving and conversing on a cellular telephone.
Simon, H. A. (1971). Designing organizations for an information-rich world.
refs.venue: In M. Greenberger (Ed.), Computers, Communication, and the Public Interest. Johns Hopkins Press

refs.title: Wikipedia: Attention economy.
Wikipedia: Inattentional blindness.
Nielsen Norman Group: Banner Blindness: Old and New Findings.
Horwitz, J. (2021). The Facebook Files.

chapters.title: Your Design Made Them Quit
chapters.subtitle: How repeated failure teaches users to give up
chapters.tldr: Learned helplessness happens when users hit failure after failure with no clear way forward. If effort keeps leading nowhere, people stop trying, even when they still want the outcome.

refs.title: Seligman, M. E. P., & Maier, S. F. (1967). Failure to escape traumatic shock.
Hiroto, D. S., & Seligman, M. E. P. (1975). Generality of learned helplessness in man.
refs.venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 31(2), 311–327

Wikipedia: HealthCare.gov.

chapters.title: You Ship What Your Boss Likes
chapters.subtitle: How authority bias overrides design judgment every time
chapters.tldr: Authority bias makes teams treat the boss's opinion as stronger evidence than it really is. Once power enters the room, people stop judging the work on its merits and start aligning to whoever can end the discussion.

refs.title: Asch, S. E. (1951). Effects of group pressure upon the modification and distortion of judgments.
refs.venue: In H. Guetzkow (Ed.), Groups, Leadership, and Men. Carnegie Press

refs.title: Milgram, S. (1963). Behavioral study of obedience.
refs.venue: Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 67(4), 371–378

refs.title: Edmondson, A. (1999). Psychological safety and learning behavior in work teams.

Wikipedia: Asch conformity experiments.
The Decision Lab: Authority Bias.
Wikipedia: Amazon Fire Phone.
Carr, A. (2015). The Real Story Behind Jeff Bezos's Fire Phone Debacle And What It Means For Amazon's Future.

chapters.title: Good Design Dies in Meetings
chapters.subtitle: How conformity and group polarization kill good work
chapters.tldr: Conformity and group polarization mean meetings do not just reveal what a team thinks. They change it. Doubts get filtered out, early opinions get amplified, and the group becomes more certain without getting more right.

refs.title: Asch, S. E. (1951). Effects of group pressure upon the modification and distortion of judgments.
Janis, I. L. (1972). Victims of Groupthink.
Moscovici, S., & Zavalloni, M. (1969). The group as a polarizer of attitudes.

refs.title: Wikipedia: Groupthink.
Simply Psychology: Asch Conformity Line Experiment.
Harvard Business Review: Making Dumb Groups Smarter (Sunstein & Hastie, 2014).
Wikipedia: New Coke.

chapters.title: A Redesign Won't Save You
chapters.subtitle: Why a redesign is usually the wrong answer
chapters.tldr: The planning fallacy makes redesigns look easier than they are, and not-invented-here bias makes the old system look worse than it is. That is why starting over feels smart even when it mostly recreates the same problems.

refs.title: Buehler, R., Griffin, D., & Ross, M. (1994). Exploring the "planning fallacy": Why people underestimate their task completion times.

chapters.title: The Metric Is Not the User
chapters.subtitle: Why optimizing for the metric stops you seeing the user
chapters.tldr: Goodhart's Law and Campbell's Law show how metrics drift away from reality. When teams chase the number hard enough, the signal stops representing the user and starts rewarding whatever moves the dashboard.

refs.title: Goodhart, C. A. E. (1975). Problems of monetary management: The U.

chapters.title: The Roadmap Ate the User
chapters.subtitle: Why teams keep building things they know aren't working
chapters.tldr: Escalation of commitment turns past decisions into future pressure. The more time, money, and reputation tied up in the roadmap, the harder it becomes to stop and admit the user needs something else.

chapters.title: Better Ship It Than Admit It
chapters.subtitle: How sunk cost turns past spend into bad decisions
chapters.tldr: The sunk cost fallacy makes teams confuse what they already spent with what users need now. The work feels too expensive to stop, so something broken gets shipped just to make the effort feel justified.

chapters.title: You Design the Same Mistakes
chapters.subtitle: Why teams keep making the same mistakes
chapters.tldr: Organizational memory does not fail all at once. It fades person by person. Once the reasoning behind old decisions is gone, teams start jogging through the same mistakes again as if they are new.

chapters.title: You Solved the Wrong Problem
chapters.subtitle: How problem framing decides what gets solved
chapters.tldr: Problem framing and problem setting decide what you notice, what you ignore, and what kind of solution even seems possible. If you accept the brief's frame too quickly, you can end up solving the wrong problem very well.

chapters.title: Research as Alibi
chapters.subtitle: How teams use research to back up what they already believe
chapters.tldr: Motivated reasoning is what turns research into an alibi. Once the team is attached to a direction, confirmation bias helps them gather, trust, and present the evidence that keeps that direction alive.

chapters.title: Now You Know Too Much
chapters.subtitle: The ethics of designing with psychological knowledge
chapters.tldr: Dark patterns are not a different kind of design. They are persuasive technology pointed the wrong way. Once you know how defaults, friction, and urgency shape behavior, you also become responsible for how you use them.
